question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: loadWithRetry(FileID, long, SystemFile)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the attempt counter (AtomicInteger attempt) is only used for logging and does not affect retry behavior, causing the retry logic to silently exhaust all 9 retries without any observable signal to the caller, ultimately returning genesis ThrottleDefinitions with permissive limits and allowing gossip of transactions that the current throttle definitions would block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that load() returns null when loadFromDB() returns null (no cache put for null results), causing the caller (FileReadableKVState.readFromDataSource) to receive null for a system file and fall back to a default behavior that does not enforce gossip throttling, allowing the attacker's transactions to bypass throttle checks entirely?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: loadAll()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that simpleFeeScheduleFile uses FeeSchedule.PROTOBUF (PBJ codec) while feeScheduleFile uses CurrentAndNextFeeSchedule.PROTOBUF (proto-java codec), and submit a request that causes the wrong codec to be applied to a fee schedule file, resulting in a ParseException that triggers retry fallback to genesis fee schedules with permissive limits, allowing gossip of transactions that should be fee-throttled?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the throttleDefinitionFile uses the defaultSystemFileCacheManager (not the exchangeRatesCacheManager) and therefore does NOT have its timestamp rounded to the hour, allowing the attacker to supply a precise nanosecond timestamp that maps to a specific historical ThrottleDefinitions version with higher per-transaction-type limits, enabling gossip of transactions beyond current throttle quotas?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: createMockAddressBook()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that createMockAddressBook() sets the node IP to '127.0.0.' + nodeId (loopback address) and port to (int) nodeId, causing the gossip layer to attempt to connect to localhost:3 for all transaction gossip, effectively preventing any transaction from being gossiped to real network peers?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the genesisNetworkProperties bypass (lines 105-107) always returns the hardcoded genesis network properties regardless of consensusTimestamp, causing the system to use genesis-era network configuration (which may have no gossip restrictions) even when the current network properties define gossip throttling rules, allowing the attacker to gossip transactions that should be blocked by current network properties?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: loadWithRetry(FileID, long, SystemFile)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that nanoSeconds is decremented by exactly 1 nanosecond (fileData.getConsensusTimestamp() - 1) on each retry to cause the retry loop to walk through a dense sequence of file_data rows (e.g., 10 consecutive corrupt FILEAPPEND rows), exhausting all retries and falling back to genesis ThrottleDefinitions, allowing gossip of transactions that the current throttle definitions would block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the CacheKey uses the rounded-down timestamp for exchange rate and fee schedule files but the original timestamp for other system files, causing a cache key mismatch where a throttle definition loaded at timestamp T is cached under key T but a subsequent request at timestamp T+1 misses the cache and loads a different (potentially more permissive) version, allowing gossip of transactions beyond current throttle limits?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: loadAll()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the systemFiles map is built using Collectors.toMap() with systemFile.genesisFile().fileId() as the key, and if two system files share the same genesis FileID (due to a misconfiguration), the map would throw an IllegalStateException during loadAll(), crashing the SystemFileLoader initialization and causing all subsequent calls to isSystemFile() and load() to fail, effectively disabling throttle enforcement and allowing gossip of all transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that load() does not validate the consensusTimestamp parameter (no bounds checking), allowing a negative timestamp value to be passed, which causes getFileAtTimestamp() to return no results (since all consensus_timestamp values are positive), triggering the genesis file fallback with permissive ThrottleDefinitions and allowing gossip of transactions that should be blocked?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: loadWithRetry(FileID, long, SystemFile)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the retryTemplate is a shared instance field (not per-request) to cause retry state interference between concurrent requests, where one request's retry exhaustion affects another request's retry count, causing the second request to fall back to genesis ThrottleDefinitions prematurely and allowing gossip of transactions that should be throttled?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the cache is not invalidated when a new ThrottleDefinitions file is written to the DB, causing the system to serve a stale (more permissive) throttle definition from cache for up to the cache TTL duration, allowing gossip of transactions that the newly-updated throttle definitions would block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: loadAll()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the mock address book is lazily initialized via @Getter(lazy=true) and createMockAddressBook() uses CommonProperties.getInstance() (a singleton), causing a race condition during first initialization where two threads read different shard/realm values from CommonProperties, resulting in an inconsistent NodeAddressBook that routes gossip to incorrect node addresses and prevents transaction propagation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the exchangeRatesCacheManager and defaultSystemFileCacheManager are separate Spring cache managers with potentially different eviction policies, causing the ThrottleDefinitions to be evicted from the default cache while a stale exchange rate is still cached, creating an inconsistency where fee calculations use old rates but throttle checks use new definitions, allowing gossip of transactions that should be blocked by the combined fee+throttle enforcement?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: loadWithRetry(FileID, long, SystemFile)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that codec.parse() is called on bytes.toReadableSequentialData() but the result is discarded, and the File is built from the original bytes, causing a scenario where a ThrottleDefinitions protobuf that is syntactically valid but semantically empty (all zero throttle buckets) passes validation and is cached, resulting in no throttle enforcement and allowing gossip of an unlimited number of transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the CacheKey.toString() method formats the timestamp as Instant.ofEpochSecond(0L, timestamp) (treating the long as nanoseconds since epoch), and if the cache implementation uses toString() for key comparison instead of equals(), two CacheKey objects with the same fileId and timestamp but different object identity could be treated as different keys, causing repeated cache misses and DB queries that load different ThrottleDefinitions versions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that load() logs at INFO level when updating the cache ('Updating cache for key {}') but does not log cache hits, making it impossible to detect a cache poisoning attack where a malicious ThrottleDefinitions entry was inserted into the cache, allowing the attacker to silently bypass gossip throttling for an extended period?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: loadAll()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the throttleDefinitionFile genesis content is loaded from fileSchema.genesisThrottleDefinitions(configuration) at startup, and if the configuration has permissive throttle buckets (e.g., very high tps limits), the genesis fallback will always allow gossip of any transaction type, making the retry-exhaustion fallback a reliable attack vector for bypassing current throttle enforcement?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/SystemFileLoader.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: load(FileID, long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the throttleDefinitionFile is not in the set of files that use exchangeRatesCacheManager (only exchangeRateFileId, feeSchedulesFileId, simpleFeeSchedulesFileId are), causing the throttle definitions to be cached with a per-request timestamp key rather than an hourly key, allowing the attacker to supply a timestamp that maps to a specific historical throttle version with higher limits and have it cached for future requests?
